title: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Addiction
subtitle: Strategies to prevent relapse
author: Ned

seo.title: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Addiction | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Addiction. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
Why does one person find public speaking easy and another person find it terrifying? According to cognitive behavioral theory, we all view the world through our core beliefs about ourselves, other people, and the world. As we encounter an opportunity like public speaking, one or more of these beliefs is activated and drives our thoughts, emotions, and behaviors. The person who is afraid of public speaking may believe that they cannot articulate their thoughts or that people won’t listen to them. “I can’t do this,” they tell themselves and their anxiety leads them to avoid the opportunity. Persons who step onto a stage with confidence have come to believe in their ability to communicate and the likelihood that others will listen. 

Persons with addiction have developed very powerful beliefs about the importance substances or addictive behaviors in their lives. They may believe that they can’t have fun, manage anxiety, make friends, or overcome sadness without their addiction. Cognitive behavioral therapy helps people modify their thoughts and behaviors and to form new beliefs about themselves and the possibility of recovery.

Once a persons has achieved a safe withdrawal from substance use, cognitive behavioral therapy helps clients prevent relapse in part by identifying both internal and external triggers that lead back to use.

  • What leads you to begin drinking? 
  • Is it a specific person, or group of people? 
  • Is it a feeling of stress, loneliness, or anger? 
  • Are you usually in a certain place when you start drinking? 
  • Is it a certain time of day? 
  • Do you see an advertisement or a wine bottle and feel the need to drink?

 

Noticing triggers helps you understand how addiction is functioning in your life. Developing strategies to manage your triggers can help you overcome the risk for relapse and achieve long-term recovery.

Substances and behaviors are not addictive because they don’t work; they are addictive because they work too well (until they stop working altogether). Once you understand the needs and desires that substances fulfill in your life, you can learn to fulfil those needs in new and healthier ways. As you do so you will gain confidence that you can live and function well without addictive substances and behaviors. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy provides one set of tools that we use to help clients change the way they think, feel, and act. Over time, these changes often help them see themselves, others, and the world in new ways.
